news_summary: NASA is inspiring global unity and curiosity this summer through engaging STEM activities and the historic Artemis II astronauts' visit to the United Nations.
news_summary_fr: Cet été, la NASA suscite un élan d'unité et de curiosité à l'échelle mondiale grâce à des activités passionnantes dans le domaine des STIM et à la visite historique des astronautes de la mission Artemis II aux Nations Unies.
news_summary_es: Este verano, la NASA está fomentando la unidad y la curiosidad a nivel mundial a través de interesantes actividades relacionadas con las ciencias, la tecnología, la ingeniería y las matemáticas (STEM), así como de la histórica visita de los astronautas de la misión Artemis II a las Naciones Unidas.

articles.title: Space Out This Summer with Variety of NASA STEM Activities
articles.summary: Summer is “Go” for launch, and NASA has a universe of ways to help you to jump in, explore, and create! Whether you prefer to spend this season fueling your creativity, going outdoors into nature, or daydreaming about your future, NASA offers ways to take your interests to the next level.  Here are some opportunities […]
articles.content:
Summer is “Go” for launch, and NASA has a universe of ways to help you to jump in, explore, and create! Whether you prefer to spend this season fueling your creativity, going outdoors into nature, or daydreaming about your future, NASA offers ways to take your interests to the next level.

Here are some opportunities to level up your skills with NASA STEM this summer.

Rise to Stardance Challenge

From Monday, June 1, through Sept. 30, students ages 13 to 18 are invited to flex their creativity in the online Stardance Challenge, a partnership between NASA and the education non-profit Hack Club. Whether you’re into space, coding, hardware, or just love building cool things, this is your chance to work with real NASA mission data from programs like Artemis, the James Webb Space Telescope, and more.

Participants can create anything from code and apps to electronics, circuit boards, models, and simulations. Hack Club will offer peer and expert reviews, prizes, and plenty of opportunities to show off your work. Meanwhile, NASA will provide access to publicly available datasets, mission materials, multimedia, and virtual sessions with subject matter experts who can share insights on space science, engineering, and careers. Ready to start brainstorming? Visit the Hack Club: Stardance Challenge website to explore project options, check out prizes, and RSVP to get a reminder when the challenge opens

NASA Astronaut Megan McArthur is conducting a technology demonstration with Astrobee flying robots. Credit: NASA

Go Behind Scenes of NASA Careers

Think NASA is only for astronauts, scientists, and tech experts? Think again. It takes a wide range of professionals and specialists to bring the nation’s aerospace goals to life. Summer is the perfect time to discover how your skills and interests could make a difference at NASA.

Connect directly with NASA experts through online events designed to spark your curiosity and help you explore real STEM career paths. These virtual sessions provide a behind‑the‑scenes look at NASA’s workforce, plus the chance to ask questions.

Tuesday, June 2: NASA’s Career Technical Education Day at Goddard Space Flight Center dives into robotics, AI, autonomous systems, and the skilled technical careers that keep NASA missions running. Register by May 26.

Thursday, June 11: Virtual Career Connection: Aviation Technology and Maintenance introduces you to aircraft mechanics and technicians who support NASA’s flight programs and explores pathways into aviation technology careers. Register by June 2.

Looking for more? Check out the Next Gen STEM for Careers web page for videos, articles, and more ways to learn about the variety of jobs at NASA.

Noctilucent clouds seen from Fairbanks, Alaska. Credit: Patrick Cobb – Photovoltaic designer, photographer

Dive into NASA Research Through Citizen Science

NASA invites people of all ages and backgrounds to do NASA science as a part of real science projects that rely on volunteers. Citizen Science is a great way to make new friends, meet some scientists, and help NASA solve mysteries of the universe this summer – using just a phone or computer. You can join from anywhere, participate on your own schedule, and dive right into real research using actual mission data. Here are two examples:

Through Space Cloud Watch, you can help NASA study noctilucent clouds. Noctilucent means “night-shining,” and that’s exactly what they do! During summer twilight at high latitudes, these clouds catch sunlight and appear to glow even in a darkened sky. Take a photo and submit a report to help scientists track how these rare clouds are changing.

Take your cloud‑watching to another planet with Cloudspotting on Mars, where you review real NASA images to identify clouds above the Red Planet and help scientists understand Martian weather.

Curious about what other projects you might enjoy? See all current Citizen Science opportunities available through NASA’s Science Mission Directorate.

No matter how you spend your summer – building projects like the Hack Club’s Stardance Challenge, jumping into real NASA research through citizen science, or exploring possible NASA career paths – there’s a launch pad waiting for you. And remember, NASA’s STEM Resources website is available year-round to serve as your one-stop hub for hands-on activities, videos, articles, and more to spark curiosity and fuel big ideas.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights NASA's broad and accessible STEM initiatives for youth and the general public, including creative challenges, career exploration, and citizen science projects. These programs provide tangible educational and inspirational opportunities that can positively impact many people by fostering skills, curiosity, and engagement with space science.
articles.personality_title: NASA offers exciting summer STEM activities for students and space fans
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every summer, many students and young people look for fun ways to learn new skills and explore their interests. NASA, the U.S. space agency, has created special programs to help people of all ages dive into science, technology, engineering, and math (STEM).

**What happened** – This summer, NASA launched several activities to get students and the public involved. One is the Stardance Challenge, running from June 1 to September 30, where teens aged 13 to 18 can use NASA mission data to create projects like apps, models, or simulations. NASA also offers virtual events where people can meet experts and learn about different careers at NASA, from robotics to aircraft maintenance. Additionally, NASA invites everyone to join citizen science projects, such as studying glowing night clouds on Earth or spotting clouds on Mars using real NASA images.

**Impact** – These activities give young people and space fans a chance to work with real NASA data and experts, helping them learn valuable skills and discover possible future careers. The citizen science projects allow anyone to contribute to real space research from home, making science more open and fun. By combining creativity, learning, and teamwork, NASA’s programs help people connect with space science in new and meaningful ways.

**What's next step** – Participants can sign up for challenges and events on NASA’s and partner websites. NASA will continue to offer these programs and update its STEM Resources website with new activities throughout the year, encouraging ongoing learning and exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – This summer, NASA invites students and space enthusiasts to explore science, develop skills, and contribute to real space research through fun and accessible STEM activities.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA propose des activités STEM passionnantes pour l’été
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque été, de nombreux élèves et jeunes cherchent des moyens amusants d’apprendre et d’explorer leurs centres d’intérêt. La NASA, l’agence spatiale américaine, a créé des programmes spéciaux pour aider tout le monde à découvrir les sciences, la technologie, l’ingénierie et les mathématiques (STEM).

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Cet été, la NASA a lancé plusieurs activités pour impliquer les étudiants et le grand public. Parmi elles, le défi Stardance, du 1er juin au 30 septembre, invite les adolescents de 13 à 18 ans à utiliser des données réelles de missions NASA pour créer des projets comme des applications, des maquettes ou des simulations. La NASA propose aussi des événements en ligne pour rencontrer des experts et découvrir différents métiers, de la robotique à la maintenance aéronautique. En plus, la NASA invite tout le monde à participer à des projets de science citoyenne, comme étudier des nuages brillants la nuit sur Terre ou repérer des nuages sur Mars avec de vraies images NASA.

**Impact** – Ces activités offrent aux jeunes et aux passionnés de l’espace la chance de travailler avec de vraies données et experts NASA, d’apprendre des compétences utiles et de découvrir des carrières possibles. Les projets de science citoyenne permettent à chacun de contribuer à la recherche spatiale depuis chez soi, rendant la science plus accessible et ludique. En combinant créativité, apprentissage et travail d’équipe, les programmes de la NASA aident les gens à se connecter à la science spatiale de manière nouvelle et concrète.

**Prochaine étape** – Les participants peuvent s’inscrire aux défis et événements sur les sites de la NASA et de ses partenaires. La NASA continuera à proposer ces programmes et à mettre à jour son site de ressources STEM avec de nouvelles activités tout au long de l’année pour encourager l’apprentissage continu.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Cet été, la NASA invite les étudiants et passionnés d’espace à explorer la science, développer des compétences et contribuer à de vraies recherches spatiales grâce à des activités STEM accessibles et amusantes.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA ofrece actividades STEM emocionantes para el verano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada verano, muchos estudiantes y jóvenes buscan formas divertidas de aprender y explorar sus intereses. NASA, la agencia espacial de Estados Unidos, ha creado programas especiales para ayudar a personas de todas las edades a descubrir la ciencia, tecnología, ingeniería y matemáticas (STEM).

**Qué pasó** – Este verano, NASA lanzó varias actividades para involucrar a estudiantes y al público general. Una es el Desafío Stardance, del 1 de junio al 30 de septiembre, donde adolescentes de 13 a 18 años pueden usar datos reales de misiones de NASA para crear proyectos como aplicaciones, modelos o simulaciones. NASA también ofrece eventos virtuales para conocer expertos y aprender sobre diferentes carreras, desde robótica hasta mantenimiento de aeronaves. Además, NASA invita a todos a unirse a proyectos de ciencia ciudadana, como estudiar nubes brillantes en la noche en la Tierra o identificar nubes en Marte usando imágenes reales de NASA.

**Impacto** – Estas actividades dan a jóvenes y aficionados al espacio la oportunidad de trabajar con datos y expertos reales de NASA, aprender habilidades importantes y descubrir posibles carreras futuras. Los proyectos de ciencia ciudadana permiten que cualquiera contribuya a la investigación espacial desde casa, haciendo la ciencia más abierta y divertida. Al combinar creatividad, aprendizaje y trabajo en equipo, los programas de NASA ayudan a las personas a conectar con la ciencia espacial de formas nuevas y significativas.

**Próximo paso** – Los participantes pueden inscribirse en los desafíos y eventos en los sitios web de NASA y sus socios. NASA continuará ofreciendo estos programas y actualizando su sitio de Recursos STEM con nuevas actividades durante todo el año, fomentando el aprendizaje continuo.

**Frase clave** – Este verano, NASA invita a estudiantes y entusiastas del espacio a explorar la ciencia, desarrollar habilidades y contribuir a investigaciones espaciales reales a través de actividades STEM divertidas y accesibles.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a glowing night sky at high latitude with shimmering noctilucent clouds above a peaceful landscape, while abstract silhouettes of young creators build futuristic models and digital devices nearby, symbolizing collaboration between youth and NASA’s space exploration, STEM challenges, and citizen science projects.

articles.title: Artemis II astronauts underscore our shared humanity, in historic UN visit
articles.summary: A month after completing their historic flyby of the Moon, the astronauts from NASA’s Artemis II mission landed at UN Headquarters in New York this week with a message that sounded like a reminder: humanity is capable of extraordinary things when it acts together.
articles.content:
Their visit on Thursday followed a long-standing tradition – cosmonauts and astronauts have been coming to the UN for decades to speak about peace, international cooperation, and our shared global future.

The four-person crew carried out the farthest human spaceflight in history, travelling beyond the far side of the Moon and safely returning to Earth after 10 intense, demanding, and inspiring days.

In that short time, as noted during the event, they “captured the imagination of billions” and rekindled a sense of shared human participation in the exploration of space.

Tradition and continuity

The General Assembly building has long welcomed pioneers of the space age, starting with Soviet cosmonauts Yuri Gagarin and Valentina Tereshkova – the first man and woman in space.

Their October 1963 visit symbolised not only technological progress but also the idea that cosmos is a domain where humanity can be united.

Since then, representatives of many nations have repeatedly addressed the international community, emphasising that space exploration is impossible without cooperation and shared responsibility.

UN Photo/YN

Artemis II continues that legacy. The mission represents not only a technological breakthrough but also a model of international partnership, involving multiple countries and institutions, including the European Space Agency, with scientific instruments and systems developed by specialists from around the world.

“I had the opportunity to talk to everyone on the way here – I mean to these three pretty normal but pretty overachieving Americans and a Canadian,” said US Ambassador Mike Waltz, who hosted the evening discussion in front of an invited audience of excited earthlings with the astronauts.

The crew stressed that their task was not only to test a spacecraft, but also to remind people on the ground that humanity can achieve great things when it works together.

Earth from deep space

For all the astronauts, the most powerful experience was seeing Earth from deep space.

From hundreds of thousands of miles away, the planet appeared small, fragile, almost weightless against the vast darkness - an image that underscored both Earth’s uniqueness and the rarity of life itself.

“I always felt urged to just be grateful for what we were seeing and to be grateful for what we were eventually going back to,” said pilot Victor Glover.

Astronaut Christina Koch described a sudden awareness of humanity’s scale within the boundless universe.

“You realize that actually there’s nothing absolute or guaranteed about this, and that actually there is such thing as a global scale…this scale is our world and what we do with it is our choice.”

© NASA

Life on board

Life inside the spacecraft was highly demanding. The crew had to balance experiments, navigation, system monitoring, and constant adaptation to microgravity.

“One day, I was opening up some granola with berries. Victor was floating kind of beside me, and just when I ripped the package, it just went too fast and it flung stuff flying across the spacecraft, and it’s like dotting his shirt.

“And he’s like, ‘Oh, don’t worry, I got this’. And he takes a spoon and he just starts eating it off of his shirt,” recalled Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen.

© NASA

A return to the Moon

Artemis II is only the beginning of a broader effort. The programme aims to return humans to the Moon, establish a sustained presence on its surface, and build infrastructure – including a lunar base – to support long-term exploration. These plans are grounded in the Artemis Accords, a set of international principles already endorsed by dozens of countries.

Speaking from the UN platform, the astronauts also addressed young people, encouraging them not to be afraid to ask questions and to listen carefully to the answers.

In closing, the crew emphasized that Artemis II was measured not only in distance or technological achievement.

Space offers a perspective that allows us to see Earth as it truly is: unique, shared, in need of care – and capable of bringing people together.

“Everyone asks what the Earth looks like from space, and most of the time I reply, the tiny Earth is an incredible place,” Reid Wiseman concluded.

Reaching for the stars

Meanwhile, a NASA aerospace engineer who grew up in Tanzania dreaming of becoming an astronaut, used to think that this was practically impossible.

Today, Alinda Mashiku is a Program Manager with the US space agency, where she helps ensure that satellites avoid collisions in orbit, contributing to the safety and sustainability of pioneering space missions such as Artemis II.

In this interview we recorded a few days ago, Ms. Mashiku told us how she encourages girls to reach for the stars:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes the historic Artemis II mission, the farthest human spaceflight, highlighting international cooperation in space exploration and its inspirational impact on humanity's shared future. It emphasizes tangible achievements, broad significance, and a hopeful message about global unity and scientific progress.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II astronauts share message of unity during historic UN visit
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A month after completing the farthest human spaceflight in history, the four astronauts from NASA’s Artemis II mission visited the United Nations (UN) Headquarters in New York. Their visit follows a long tradition where space travelers come to the UN to talk about peace, cooperation, and our shared future on Earth.

**What happened** – The Artemis II crew traveled beyond the far side of the Moon and returned safely after 10 days in space. At the UN, they spoke about their mission and the importance of working together. They shared stories about life aboard the spacecraft, including challenges like floating food, and described the powerful experience of seeing Earth from deep space—a small, fragile planet surrounded by vast darkness.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it shows how countries can work together on big projects like space exploration. The Artemis II mission involved not just NASA but also the European Space Agency and experts from many countries. The astronauts’ visit reminded people that space exploration is more than science; it helps us see Earth as one planet that needs care and cooperation. Their stories inspired people, especially young listeners, to stay curious and work together.

**What’s next step** – Artemis II is the start of a larger plan to send humans back to the Moon and build a permanent base there. This will help scientists explore space for longer times and prepare for future missions, possibly to Mars. The program follows international agreements called the Artemis Accords, which many countries have signed to ensure peaceful and cooperative space exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Artemis II mission and its UN visit highlight how international teamwork in space exploration can inspire us to protect and unite our shared home, Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d’Artemis II prônent l’unité lors d’une visite historique à l’ONU
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un mois après avoir accompli le plus long vol spatial habité de l’histoire, les quatre astronautes de la mission Artemis II de la NASA ont visité le siège des Nations Unies (ONU) à New York. Cette visite s’inscrit dans une longue tradition où les voyageurs de l’espace viennent à l’ONU pour parler de paix, de coopération et de notre avenir commun sur Terre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’équipage d’Artemis II a voyagé au-delà de la face cachée de la Lune et est revenu sain et sauf après 10 jours dans l’espace. À l’ONU, ils ont parlé de leur mission et de l’importance de travailler ensemble. Ils ont partagé des anecdotes sur la vie à bord, comme les défis de la nourriture en apesanteur, et ont décrit l’expérience forte de voir la Terre depuis l’espace lointain — une planète petite et fragile entourée d’un vaste vide.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car elle montre comment les pays peuvent coopérer sur de grands projets comme l’exploration spatiale. Artemis II a impliqué non seulement la NASA mais aussi l’Agence spatiale européenne et des experts de nombreux pays. La visite des astronautes a rappelé que l’exploration spatiale est plus qu’un défi scientifique ; elle nous aide à voir la Terre comme une planète unique qui a besoin d’attention et de coopération. Leurs récits ont inspiré les gens, surtout les jeunes, à rester curieux et à travailler ensemble.

**Prochaine étape** – Artemis II est le début d’un plus grand projet visant à renvoyer des humains sur la Lune et à construire une base permanente. Cela aidera les scientifiques à explorer l’espace sur de plus longues périodes et à préparer des missions futures, peut-être vers Mars. Le programme suit des accords internationaux appelés les Accords Artemis, signés par de nombreux pays pour garantir une exploration spatiale pacifique et coopérative.

**Phrase-clé** – La mission Artemis II et sa visite à l’ONU montrent comment la coopération internationale dans l’exploration spatiale peut nous inspirer à protéger et unir notre maison commune, la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II destacan la unidad durante histórica visita a la ONU
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un mes después de completar el vuelo espacial tripulado más lejano en la historia, los cuatro astronautas de la misión Artemis II de la NASA visitaron la sede de las Naciones Unidas (ONU) en Nueva York. Esta visita sigue una larga tradición donde los viajeros espaciales vienen a la ONU para hablar de paz, cooperación y nuestro futuro compartido en la Tierra.

**Qué pasó** – La tripulación de Artemis II viajó más allá del lado oculto de la Luna y regresó sana y salva tras 10 días en el espacio. En la ONU, hablaron sobre su misión y la importancia de trabajar juntos. Compartieron historias sobre la vida a bordo, incluyendo desafíos como la comida flotante, y describieron la experiencia poderosa de ver la Tierra desde el espacio profundo, un planeta pequeño y frágil rodeado de una vasta oscuridad.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque muestra cómo los países pueden colaborar en grandes proyectos como la exploración espacial. Artemis II involucró no solo a la NASA, sino también a la Agencia Espacial Europea y expertos de muchos países. La visita de los astronautas recordó que la exploración espacial es más que ciencia; nos ayuda a ver la Tierra como un planeta único que necesita cuidado y cooperación. Sus relatos inspiraron a las personas, especialmente a los jóvenes, a mantener la curiosidad y a trabajar juntos.

**Próximo paso** – Artemis II es el comienzo de un plan más amplio para enviar humanos de regreso a la Luna y construir una base permanente allí. Esto ayudará a los científicos a explorar el espacio por períodos más largos y prepararse para futuras misiones, posiblemente a Marte. El programa sigue acuerdos internacionales llamados los Acuerdos Artemis, firmados por muchos países para garantizar una exploración espacial pacífica y cooperativa.

**Frase clave** – La misión Artemis II y su visita a la ONU muestran cómo el trabajo en equipo internacional en la exploración espacial puede inspirarnos a proteger y unir nuestro hogar compartido, la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of four glowing, interconnected orbs orbiting a softly illuminated crescent Moon above a fragile, small Earth seen from deep space, all set against a vast, dark cosmic background; the orbs symbolize astronauts united in peaceful international cooperation, with subtle hints of scientific instruments and flowing lines representing shared human curiosity and exploration.
